content.text: is a short, structured document that captures a single significant architectural decision — what was decided, why it was decided that way, and what the expected consequences are. The format was popularised by Michael Nygard in 2011 and has since become an industry norm for tracking the reasoning behind technical choices alongside the code that implements them. When applied to agentic AI and GenAI systems, the ADR format gains additional AI-specific fields: the capabilities and limitations of candidate models, token economics, latency budgets, compliance constraints around data residency, and the often-irreversible nature of prompt-architecture commitments.
ADRs for GenAI systems live in a
folder in the same git repository as the codebase. Each file is numbered sequentially —

— and is treated as an append-only log. Decisions that are superseded or deprecated are never deleted; instead, a new ADR is written that references the old one, preserving the full decision history in
. This immutability is what makes ADRs trustworthy: you can always trace why the system looks the way it does today.
The Nygard template defines five mandatory fields:
. For GenAI workloads, extended templates add
— because model capabilities and pricing change fast enough that a decision accepted today may need revisiting in twelve months.

Agentic AI systems accumulate decisions at a pace that outstrips most traditional software projects. Within a few sprints, a team will have chosen an LLM provider and specific model, selected an embedding model and vector database, decided whether to use RAG or fine-tuning, committed to an agent orchestration framework, and locked in a system-prompt architecture. Each of these choices has downstream implications that compound: the vector store selected in week two shapes the retrieval latency budget in week six, which then constrains the model's maximum context window usage in week ten. Without ADRs, these dependencies become invisible tribal knowledge that evaporates when the original engineer moves on.
The cost of undocumented decisions in GenAI systems is particularly high because many AI choices are

. If a team fine-tuned a model on proprietary data, that training run is expensive to repeat with different hyperparameters. If the vector store was chosen without documenting why competing options were rejected, the team cannot easily evaluate whether switching to a new option six months later would address their current scalability problems or simply reproduce them. ADRs prevent this amnesia by making the reasoning durable.

Consider a concrete example: a team chose to use retrieval-augmented generation rather than fine-tuning for a customer-support agent. The ADR captured that the deciding factors were (1) the knowledge base changes weekly, making static fine-tuning stale within days, (2) the compliance team required that no customer conversations be used as training data, and (3) the infrastructure budget did not support a weekly fine-tuning pipeline. Eighteen months later, a new engineer proposes fine-tuning to improve response quality. The ADR is read in five minutes; the compliance and freshness constraints are still valid; the proposal is refined rather than abandoned. Without the ADR, the team would have spent weeks re-learning the same constraints.
Every architectural choice is a first-class artifact in git.
shows the exact date, author, and PR where each decision was accepted.
New engineers can read ADR-001 through ADR-010 in a single afternoon and understand why the system looks the way it does, without needing to interrogate senior colleagues.
Teams can enforce a review process (pull request, two approvers) for ADRs without heavyweight architecture review boards. The record is the committee meeting.
field in ADRs creates a natural schedule for revisiting model and tool choices as the GenAI landscape evolves.
ADRs that record token economics, latency budgets, and data-residency constraints provide auditors with a clear narrative of how AI-related compliance decisions were made.
Capturing the cost and difficulty of reversing a decision helps teams understand which ADRs need the most deliberation before acceptance.
ADRs that are never updated become misleading. Teams must build a habit of writing a superseding ADR when a previous decision changes, rather than just editing code.
Nothing in the build pipeline prevents engineers from making architectural changes without a corresponding ADR. Adoption requires team discipline or a PR review checklist.
A full AI-extended ADR template can take 30–60 minutes to write well. Teams under delivery pressure may skip ADRs for decisions that feel small but compound later.

Teams often struggle to decide which decisions warrant an ADR. A useful heuristic: if the decision would be hard to reverse, costly to misunderstand, or affects more than one system component, write the ADR.
An ADR moves through a lifecycle of statuses. It starts as

— written by a team member who has identified a decision that needs to be made. It is then opened as a pull request, where reviewers discuss the context section and push back on the alternatives-considered section. Once the team reaches consensus, the status field is changed to
and the PR is merged. The merge commit timestamp becomes the official decision date.
When circumstances change — a better model becomes available, a vendor is acquired, a new compliance requirement emerges — the team writes a new ADR. The old ADR's status is changed to
. It is never deleted. This immutability means the complete decision history is always accessible in

section should include: the model capabilities that are relevant (context window size, function-calling support, multimodality), token cost per 1K input/output tokens, expected request volume and the implied monthly cost, latency requirements (P95 target), and any data-residency or privacy constraints that eliminate certain providers. The
section should separately enumerate positive and negative consequences, including the cost of reversing the decision if it turns out to be wrong.

attrs.alt: ADR repository architecture: ADR Git Repository at top feeds ADR Documents (left) and Review Workflow (right). A central Solution Architecture box links down to four components — Model Layer, Agent Framework, RAG/Memory, and Prompt Architecture — each labelled with the ADR IDs that govern it. A status legend shows Proposed, Under Review, Accepted, and Deprecated.
attrs.title: ADR Repository Architecture for Agentic AI Systems

content.text: The diagram shows how a single ADR Git Repository serves as the source of truth. ADR documents (YAML or Markdown files) are stored in
alongside a review workflow driven by pull requests: a new ADR begins as
when a PR is opened, and becomes

on merge. Each component of the solution architecture — Model Layer, Agent Framework, RAG/Memory, Prompt Architecture — references the ADR IDs that govern it, creating bidirectional traceability: from code to decision and from decision to code.

Write an ADR whenever a decision meets any one of these criteria: it affects more than one component or team; it is difficult or expensive to reverse; it involves a trade-off between two or more viable options; it has non-obvious compliance, cost, or latency implications; or it is the kind of choice a future engineer would question and need to re-investigate if the rationale is lost.
choosing between Claude 3.5 Sonnet and GPT-4o for the primary reasoning model, or selecting a smaller model for a high-volume classification step.
choosing between pgvector, Pinecone, Weaviate, or Qdrant. The schema migration cost of switching later is high.
committing to LangGraph, LlamaIndex, AutoGen, or a bespoke orchestration loop. Framework lock-in is real; document why the selected framework fits the team's requirements.
this is one of the highest-impact decisions in a GenAI system. The choice affects freshness, compliance, infrastructure cost, and the ability to update the knowledge base.
decisions about system prompt structure, chain-of-thought strategies, tool schemas, and how context is assembled for each turn.
hard limits on max_tokens, context compression approaches, and policies for when to summarise vs truncate conversation history.
directory in your repository. Add a
explaining the numbering scheme and the review process.

Choose a format: YAML is machine-readable and lends itself to tooling and CI validation; Markdown is more human-readable in GitHub and PR reviews. Both are fine; YAML is recommended for GenAI ADRs where the structured fields (token_cost, latency_budget_ms) benefit from a schema.
, etc. Do not reuse numbers. Add a short slug in the filename:

Open a pull request for each new ADR. Require at least one review from a non-author team member. The PR description is a good place for the initial discussion; the ADR document should capture the final settled position, not the debate.
Add ADR references to code. In the relevant module's docstring or
. This makes the link from code to decision explicit.
field to each ADR. Create a recurring calendar event or a CI job that opens a GitHub issue when an ADR's review date passes.

# ADR-001: Primary Reasoning Model Selection
# docs/decisions/ADR-001-model-selection.yaml

id: ADR-001
title: "Primary Reasoning Model: Claude 3.5 Sonnet"
status: accepted            # proposed | accepted | deprecated | superseded
date: "2025-03-14"
authors:
  - alice@example.com
  - bob@example.com
review_date: "2026-03-14"   # Revisit annually; model landscape changes fast
supersedes: null
superseded_by: null

context: |
  The agent requires multi-step reasoning over 50-100 retrieved chunks
  (avg 200 tokens each), tool-call orchestration, and structured JSON output.

  Evaluation criteria:
    - Context window: minimum 100K tokens required
    - Function-calling: required (native JSON schema tool use)
    - Latency: P95 < 4 s for a 3-turn reasoning chain
    - Cost: < /bin/bash.02 per agent invocation at expected volume (10K/day)
    - Data residency: no EU personal data may leave EU region

  Candidates evaluated:
    - claude-3-5-sonnet-20241022  (Anthropic)
    - gpt-4o-2024-11-20            (OpenAI)
    - gemini-1.5-pro-002           (Google)

ai_constraints:
  context_window_tokens: 200000
  input_cost_per_1k_tokens: 0.003
  output_cost_per_1k_tokens: 0.015
  latency_p95_ms: 3800
  function_calling: native
  data_residency: "EU (aws-eu-west-1 via Anthropic API)"

decision: |
  Use claude-3-5-sonnet-20241022 as the primary reasoning model.

  Rationale:
    1. Highest score on internal eval set (tool-calling accuracy 91% vs 87% GPT-4o).
    2. 200K context window eliminates the need for context truncation at current chunk volumes.
    3. EU data-residency available via Anthropic's AWS EU deployment.
    4. Native JSON schema tool use reduces prompt engineering for structured outputs.

consequences:
  positive:
    - Tool-calling reliability reduces hallucinated function invocations.
    - 200K window removes need for a context-compression step in the current pipeline.
    - Single-vendor contract simplifies compliance reporting.
  negative:
    - Vendor lock-in: switching requires rewriting tool schemas and re-running evals.
    - Anthropic pricing changes would require a new ADR and potential re-evaluation.
    - No open-source fallback; service disruption has no immediate on-prem alternative.

alternatives_considered:
  - model: gpt-4o-2024-11-20
    rejected_because: |
      Tool-calling accuracy 4 pp lower on internal eval. OpenAI EU data residency
      requires additional contract rider not yet signed by legal.
  - model: gemini-1.5-pro-002
    rejected_because: |
      Latency P95 was 6.2 s in benchmarks, exceeding the 4 s budget.
      Native function calling schema differs from OpenAI-compatible format used
      by existing tool definitions.

# ADR-003: Vector Store for RAG Knowledge Base
# docs/decisions/ADR-003-vector-store.yaml

id: ADR-003
title: "Vector Store: pgvector on PostgreSQL"
status: accepted
date: "2025-03-21"
authors:
  - carol@example.com
review_date: "2026-03-21"
supersedes: null
superseded_by: null

context: |
  The RAG pipeline requires approximate nearest-neighbour search over
  ~2M document chunks (768-dim embeddings from text-embedding-3-small).

  Non-functional requirements:
    - Query latency: P95 < 80 ms for top-20 retrieval
    - Write throughput: up to 5K chunk upserts/hour during ingestion jobs
    - Hosting: must run in existing AWS RDS infrastructure (no new managed services)
    - ACID: transactional consistency between chunk metadata and embeddings required

ai_constraints:
  embedding_dimensions: 768
  corpus_size_chunks: 2000000
  retrieval_top_k: 20
  latency_budget_ms: 80

decision: |
  Use pgvector extension on the existing PostgreSQL 16 RDS instance.

  Rationale:
    1. Already-approved infrastructure — no new vendor procurement cycle.
    2. ACID guarantees mean metadata updates and embedding upserts are atomic.
    3. At 2M chunks with HNSW index, P95 retrieval is 45 ms — well within budget.
    4. Avoids dual-write complexity of maintaining a separate vector store and
       relational database for chunk metadata.

consequences:
  positive:
    - Zero new infrastructure cost; runs on existing RDS instance.
    - Single source of truth for both metadata and vectors.
    - Standard SQL for hybrid search (vector similarity + metadata filters).
  negative:
    - Scaling beyond ~50M chunks will require vertical RDS scaling or partitioning.
    - pgvector HNSW is approximate; recall@20 is ~97%, not 100%.
    - Operational team must learn pgvector index tuning (ef_construction, m parameters).

alternatives_considered:
  - tool: Pinecone (managed)
    rejected_because: |
      Requires data leaving VPC; EU data residency not available on Starter plan.
      Additional monthly cost (0–00/month depending on pod size).
  - tool: Weaviate (self-hosted)
    rejected_because: |
      Requires Kubernetes deployment not currently in operations runbook.
      Separate cluster adds operational complexity and a new failure domain.
ADR Template: RAG vs Fine-tuning Decision (YAML)

# ADR-005: Knowledge Integration Strategy — RAG vs Fine-tuning
# docs/decisions/ADR-005-rag-vs-fine-tuning.yaml

id: ADR-005
title: "Knowledge Integration: RAG over Fine-tuning"
status: accepted
date: "2025-04-02"
authors:
  - dave@example.com
review_date: "2025-10-02"   # Shorter cycle: fine-tuning costs dropping fast

context: |
  The customer-support agent must answer questions about product documentation
  that is updated weekly. Two strategies were evaluated:

  Option A — Retrieval-Augmented Generation (RAG):
    Store documentation chunks in pgvector; retrieve relevant chunks per query;
    inject into context window at inference time.

  Option B — Supervised Fine-tuning (SFT):
    Fine-tune a base model on product documentation and support Q&A pairs.
    Repeat weekly as docs change.

  Compliance constraint: customer conversation data cannot be used as training
  data (DPA clause 4.2). Fine-tuning on support conversations is prohibited.

  Cost estimate (fine-tuning):
    Weekly SFT run: ~00 compute + 00 human review = ,000/week = 2K/year.

ai_constraints:
  knowledge_freshness_required: weekly
  training_data_compliance_constraint: "No customer PII in training corpus (DPA 4.2)"
  estimated_fine_tuning_cost_usd_per_year: 52000

decision: |
  Use RAG. Knowledge base updated via nightly ingestion job.

consequences:
  positive:
    - Documentation changes are live within 24 hours of commit.
    - Zero training cost; no compliance exposure from customer data.
    - Retrieval provides citations that improve response trustworthiness.
  negative:
    - Retrieval quality depends on embedding model and chunking strategy.
    - Context window consumed by retrieved chunks reduces space for reasoning.
    - Hallucinations possible if retrieval returns irrelevant chunks.

alternatives_considered:
  - strategy: Fine-tuning (SFT)
    rejected_because: |
      2K/year cost. Weekly cadence required. Compliance prohibition on using
      customer conversations as training data. Static knowledge degrades between
      training runs (7-day freshness gap unacceptable for fast-changing product).
  - strategy: LoRA adapters on self-hosted model
    rejected_because: |
      Self-hosted model infrastructure not approved. GPU cluster procurement
      requires 3-month lead time. LoRA still cannot use customer conversation data.

# ADR-006: System Prompt Architecture
# docs/decisions/ADR-006-prompt-architecture.yaml

id: ADR-006
title: "System Prompt Structure: Role + Rules + Tool Guidance"
status: accepted
date: "2025-04-10"
authors:
  - eve@example.com
review_date: "2025-10-10"

context: |
  The agent's system prompt governs persona, safety rails, tool invocation
  guidance, and output format. Three structural approaches were evaluated:

  A — Flat prose: a single paragraph defining role and rules.
  B — Numbered rules list: enumerated constraints with explicit priorities.
  C — Section-based: Role | Constraints | Tool Guidance | Output Format sections.

  Evaluation metrics: rule-following score on 150-query eval set,
  instruction-conflict rate, token cost.

decision: |
  Use section-based system prompt (Option C) with explicit XML-style section
  delimiters: <role>, <constraints>, <tool_guidance>, <output_format>.

  Rationale:
    1. Section-based scored 94% rule-following vs 81% flat prose on eval set.
    2. XML delimiters allow programmatic extraction of sections for A/B testing
       individual sections without regenerating the full prompt.
    3. Token cost: 420 tokens per request — 80 tokens more than flat prose but
       within the 500-token system-prompt budget.

consequences:
  positive:
    - High rule-following score reduces safety-rail violations.
    - Sections can be versioned and A/B tested independently.
    - Clear structure makes prompt reviews faster for new team members.
  negative:
    - 80 extra tokens per request at 10K requests/day = 800K tokens/day extra cost.
    - XML delimiters may interact unexpectedly with models not trained on this style.

alternatives_considered:
  - approach: Flat prose
    rejected_because: "81% rule-following — 13 pp below section-based on eval set."
  - approach: Numbered rules list
    rejected_because: |
      92% rule-following (2 pp below section-based). Poor at expressing
      conditional rules (if context X then constraint Y) without verbose
      numbering schemes that consume extra tokens.

A fintech startup building an AI-assisted document-review agent accumulated 12 ADRs in their first six weeks. When a new model (claude-3-5-haiku) was released at one-tenth the cost, an engineer proposed switching the reasoning model. ADR-001 was read in ten minutes: it showed the original evaluation was performed before claude-3-5-haiku existed, that the latency budget was 4 s P95, and that tool-calling accuracy on their internal eval set was the deciding factor.

The team ran the same 150-query eval on the new model in two hours. claude-3-5-haiku scored 88% (vs 91% for Sonnet) with P95 latency of 1.2 s. For their use case — document pre-screening with a human reviewer in the loop — 88% accuracy was acceptable given the 10x cost saving. They wrote ADR-013, which superseded ADR-001, capturing the new evaluation results, the revised cost-quality trade-off, and the decision to use Haiku for the pre-screening step while retaining Sonnet for the final synthesis step. The change was approved and merged in the same afternoon. The entire decision history is in
, traceable to the PR and the engineer who wrote the ADR.

ADRs written weeks or months after a decision was implemented are mostly reconstructed from memory and lack the actual alternatives that were seriously considered at the time. The value of an ADR comes from capturing the decision in the moment, when the context is fresh and the trade-offs are real. If your process is to code first and document later, ADRs will be superficial.
The most common omission is a weak or absent

section. If you only document what was chosen, you lose the why — specifically, why the other options were not chosen. In six months, when someone asks "did you evaluate Pinecone?", the answer should be in the ADR, not in someone's memory.

Teams sometimes edit an accepted ADR to reflect a changed decision rather than writing a new one. This destroys the history: you can no longer see what was decided originally, when it changed, and why. Always write a new ADR that references the old one via
, and update the old ADR's status field to

ADRs for GenAI systems that use generic software-architecture templates miss the fields that matter most: token economics, latency measurements, eval scores, and compliance constraints. A model selection ADR that simply says "we chose Claude because it has good reasoning" is nearly useless; one that records the specific eval scores, cost per request, and the data-residency constraint that eliminated a competitor is genuinely useful six months later.
The PR that introduces the decision should include the ADR. If the ADR is controversial, resolve the controversy in the ADR PR before writing the implementation.
Every model selection ADR should include token costs, measured latency (not theoretical), eval scores, and context window requirements. Qualitative descriptions age poorly.
comments in the relevant module. When an engineer reads the code and wonders why pgvector was chosen over Pinecone, a one-line comment takes them to the full reasoning.
Set review dates for fast-moving areas.
Model selection and token economics change quickly. ADRs in these areas should have a
within 6–12 months. Infrastructure decisions change more slowly and can have 2-year review dates.
A good ADR is 300–600 words. If the context section runs to 1,000 words, it is probably two decisions that should be two ADRs.
Write a JSON Schema for your ADR YAML format and run
+ schema validation in CI. This catches missing required fields (e.g., a
omitted for a model selection ADR) before the PR merges.

In the consequences section, explicitly state how hard it would be to undo this decision. "Reversing this requires re-embedding 2M documents (estimated 8 hours, 20 API cost)" is more useful than "reversal is possible but costly".
ADRs are plaintext files; they scale to thousands of entries with no tooling overhead. The main scalability concern is
: as the ADR count grows past 50, engineers need to be able to find the relevant ADR quickly. Maintaining an

that maps ADR IDs to one-line summaries and status helps. A structured YAML format also allows tooling such as adr-tools or custom scripts to generate HTML indexes, status dashboards, and review-date reminders from the raw files.
script that reads all YAML ADRs, checks required fields, and verifies that supersedes/superseded_by cross-references are internally consistent can be run in under 1 second even for 200 ADRs.

A weekly GitHub Actions job can generate a Markdown summary of all ADRs grouped by status (accepted, deprecated, proposed) and post it to an internal wiki, giving the team a live view of the decision landscape.
Since ADRs are plaintext in git,
or GitHub's code search finds every ADR that mentions a technology in milliseconds.
on GitHub) that creates, lists, and links ADRs from the command line. Uses Markdown format. Widely used in the original Nygard tradition.
A Node.js tool that renders a searchable ADR knowledge base as a static web site from Markdown ADR files. Integrates with GitHub Actions for automatic deployment.
Provides in-editor creation, status updates, and navigation between ADRs and the code that references them.
validates required fields. Both run in CI in milliseconds.
file can scaffold the ADR checklist for PR authors: have all alternatives been considered? Is a review date set? Is the ADR cross-referenced in the relevant code?

ADRs fit between the brevity of an in-code comment and the weight of a full design document or RFC. For GenAI systems where many smaller but consequential decisions accumulate quickly — model choice, embedding strategy, context-window management, tool schemas — the lightweight ADR format hits the right balance of documentation quality versus engineering overhead.

ADR YAML files may contain sensitive information: API endpoint URLs, cost per token (which could reveal negotiated pricing), compliance constraints that expose internal legal positions, or the names of rejected vendors with reasons that could be commercially sensitive. Treat
as an internal document — in a private repository, access-controlled the same way as the codebase.
Never embed API keys, credentials, or bearer tokens in ADR files, even as examples. Use placeholder values such as
The append-only nature of ADRs means that the compliance team can rely on the decision history being intact. Branch protection rules on the
path prevent force-pushes that could erase history.
ADRs that document data-residency decisions, PII handling, or training data policies should have a legal or compliance reviewer as a required approver in the GitHub CODEOWNERS file.

For model-selection ADRs, record the vendor's SLA (uptime guarantee, rate limits, support tier) in the context section. This creates an audit record of the reliability assumptions made at decision time.

tells you when each line of code was written, why it was changed, and who approved it, an ADR repository tells you when each architectural choice was made, what problem it solved, and what alternatives were rejected. The code shows you the
For agentic AI systems in particular, the

is especially fragile. Model capabilities, pricing, and compliance requirements change faster than in traditional software. An ADR that records today's token economics will look outdated in twelve months — but that is the point. The outdated ADR with a
is a prompt to re-evaluate, not evidence of neglect. A missing ADR means the team cannot tell whether a decision was deliberate, accidental, or just forgotten.
An ADR captures a single architectural decision with its context, the rationale for the choice, alternatives considered, and consequences — making the reasoning durable and discoverable in git.
GenAI ADRs extend the Nygard template with AI-specific fields: token economics, latency measurements, eval scores, model capabilities, and compliance constraints that restrict vendor selection.
ADRs are append-only: a superseded decision is never deleted; a new ADR is written that references it, preserving the full decision history in git log.
Write ADRs for model selection, vector store choice, agent framework, RAG vs fine-tuning, and prompt architecture — any decision that is hard to reverse or has non-obvious downstream implications.
field in every GenAI ADR. Model pricing, capabilities, and compliance requirements change on a 6–12 month cycle, and a review date creates a systematic reminder to re-evaluate.
in the code module it governs, and reference the code module in the ADR's context section.
Validate YAML ADRs in CI with a schema check; a missing required field (such as
on a model selection ADR) should be a CI failure, not a review comment.
ADRs are the git log for your architecture: the code shows what was built, the ADRs explain why it was built that way and what was deliberately not built.
